basicPropertyValues.val: PMD Core application ontology generated via ODK Template.

comments: This role is realized when the material enters the supply chain or the hopper of a processing machine.

basicPropertyValues.val: A role borne by a material entity when it functions as an input to a manufacturing process prior to transformation or processing.
Plastic granules sitting in a silo waiting to be fed into an extruder bear this role.

comments: Additives are distinct from fillers (which bulk up volume) and the matrix. They perform specific chemical or physical modifications.

basicPropertyValues.val: A role borne by a substance intentionally added in relatively small amounts to modify the processing characteristics, performance properties, or cost of a base material.
UV stabilizers added to dashboard polymers to prevent sun damage; Plasticizers added to PVC.

comments: Organic colorants (dyes) offer high color strength and brightness but may have lower thermal stability than pigments.

basicPropertyValues.val: A complex organic chemical compound used to impart color, typically soluble in the medium.
Azo dyes used in interior textile coloring.

comments: Inorganic colorants (pigments) are generally insoluble and provide opacity and high heat stability.

basicPropertyValues.val: A mineral-based chemical compound, usually an oxide or sulfide, used to impart color.
Titanium dioxide (white) used in car paints.

comments: This role can be borne by organic dyes or inorganic pigments.

basicPropertyValues.val: A role borne by an additive substance when its primary function is to impart color to a material.
Carbon black acting as a pigment to turn a tire black.

comments: For brittle materials, this is equal to tensile strength. For ductile materials, it may be lower than the yield point.

basicPropertyValues.val: The tensile stress recorded at the moment of rupture of the specimen.
A value of 150 MPa for a GF-reinforced polymer.

comments: Can be defined descriptively (Red) or metrologically (L*a*b* coordinates).

basicPropertyValues.val: The quality of an object or substance with respect to light reflected by the object, usually determined visually by measurement of hue, saturation, and brightness.
The 'Piano Black' finish on a B-pillar trim.

comments: Polyamides are semi-crystalline polymers known for good thermal and chemical resistance.

basicPropertyValues.val: A polymer containing repeating amide (-CONH-) linkages in the polymer backbone.

comments: PA66 has a higher melting point (~260°C) than PA6 but absorbs moisture which affects dimensional stability.

basicPropertyValues.val: A semicrystalline thermoplastic polyamide synthesized from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid.
Material used for cylinder head covers and radiator end tanks.

comments: The addition of 25% glass fiber significantly increases stiffness and tensile strength compared to neat PA66.

basicPropertyValues.val: A composite material consisting of a Polyamide 66 matrix reinforced with 25% glass fiber by mass.
Structural components in the engine bay requiring high stiffness at elevated temperatures.

comments: In PMD logic, this is a disconnected aggregate because the individual filaments in a bundle are distinct objects.

basicPropertyValues.val: A class of materials that are continuous filaments or are in discrete elongated pieces, similar to lengths of thread.

comments: Used extensively as a reinforcement agent in polymer matrix composites.

basicPropertyValues.val: A material consisting of numerous extremely fine fibers of glass.
E-Glass used to reinforce PA66 for automotive pedals.

comments: Commonly denoted as wt%. PA66-GF25 implies a value of 0.25 or 25%.

basicPropertyValues.val: The ratio of the mass of the fiber reinforcement to the total mass of the composite material.

comments: Volume fraction is more critical for micromechanical modeling (e.g., Rule of Mixtures) than mass fraction.

basicPropertyValues.val: The ratio of the volume of the fiber reinforcement to the total volume of the composite material.
Approximately 14-15% volume fraction corresponds to 25% mass fraction for Glass/PA66.
A unique string or code assigned by a manufacturer to identify a specific product grade.
BASF code 'A3WG6' for a specific Ultramid grade.
The brand or trade name used for marketing the material.
A string following a standard nomenclature (e.g., ISO 1043) to describe the material composition.
A globally unique identifier (GUID) or database key for the material entry.
Code assigned to a specific production run for traceability.
Unique identifier for a legal entity in the automotive supply chain network (Catena-X standard).

comments: Indicates ductility. Low strain at break (less than 5%) suggests brittle behavior.

basicPropertyValues.val: The elongation of a specimen at the moment of rupture, expressed as a percentage of the original gauge length.
3% strain at break for a glass-filled polyamide.

comments: Critical for injection molding simulations to predict mold filling.

basicPropertyValues.val: A measure of a fluid's resistance to flow.
The melt viscosity of polypropylene at 230°C.

comments: The tool that imparts the shape to the engineered material during the primary shaping process.

basicPropertyValues.val: A hollowed-out block that is filled with a liquid or pliable material like plastic, glass, metal, or ceramic raw material.
Injection mold for a dashboard panel.

comments: Polymers are non-Newtonian; their shear viscosity decreases as shear rate increases (shear thinning).

basicPropertyValues.val: The viscosity of a fluid when subjected to shear stress; defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear rate.
Viscosity curve data required for Moldflow simulation.

comments: Below Tg, the amorphous phase is frozen; above Tg, polymer chains have mobility.

basicPropertyValues.val: The temperature range where a polymer material transitions from a hard, glassy material to a soft, rubbery material.
Tg of approx. 50°C for dry PA66.

comments: Crucial for calculating gap and flush in automotive assembly where different materials (steel vs plastic) meet.

basicPropertyValues.val: The fractional change in length per degree of temperature change.
CLTE is distinct in flow-direction vs cross-flow direction for fiber reinforced parts.

comments: Used to calculate cooling times in injection molding.

basicPropertyValues.val: The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a substance per unit of mass.

comments: Often measured via Charpy or Izod notched impact tests.

basicPropertyValues.val: The ability of a material to absorb energy and resist fracture under high speed loading.
High impact strength is required for bumper beams.

comments: Standard metric for stiffness in plastics (ISO 178).

basicPropertyValues.val: The ratio of stress to strain in flexural deformation, or the tendency for a material to resist bending.
Flexural modulus of 8500 MPa for PA66-GF30.

comments: Relevant for snap-fit joints in automotive interiors.

basicPropertyValues.val: The maximum stress a material can withstand in bending before yielding or breaking.

comments: Crucial for PA66 as it absorbs moisture, changing its mechanical properties (conditioning).

basicPropertyValues.val: The concentration of water vapor present in the air or environment surrounding a material.

nodes.lbl: 2. light alloys, cast and wrought alloys
3. nonferrous heavy metals, cast and wrought alloys

basicPropertyValues.val: Standardized material classification system used in the German automotive industry.

nodes.lbl: 1.2.1 cast iron with lamellar graphite / tempered cast iron
1.2.2 cast iron with nodular graphite / vermicular cast iron
